refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined design of propulsion, control, and operational strategies, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a data interface (telemetry, dashboard, or log) that reports: (a) total energy consumed (kWh or equivalent) over a defined evaluation period, and (b) total work output (tons moved) over the same period, with sufficient granularity to compute energy_per_ton. Confirm the measurement methodology aligns with the agreed evaluation protocol.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall measure and record total energy consumed during operation.
The NGHE solution shall measure and record total work output (mass moved) during operation.
The NGHE solution shall compute and expose the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) to authorized stakeholders.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Deploy real-time energy and tonnage sensors with continuous data aggregation; compute energy_per_ton at configurable intervals; expose metric via reporting interface

DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module
DP4: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs and work logs (manual or semi-automated); reconcile and compute energy_per_ton through periodic audit process

DP5: Decompose into two independent measurement streams—(a) energy consumption measurement with configurable granularity, (b) work output quantification with independent sensor/logging—then compose via a neutral aggregation and reporting layer that accepts both streams and computes the ratio
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Decompose into two independent measurement streams—(a) energy consumption measurement with configurable granularity, (b) work output quantification with independent sensor/logging—then compose via a neutral aggregation and reporting layer that accepts both streams and computes the ratio

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage streams (mitigated by aggregation layer accepting time-windowed data)
Sensor calibration drift affecting both streams (mitigated by independent validation and recalibration protocols)
Reporting interface assumptions about data freshness and latency (mitigated by explicit SLA definition in aggregation layer contract)
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves low coupling by treating energy measurement and work quantification as orthogonal concerns. Each stream can evolve independently (e.g., upgrade energy sensor without touching tonnage logic). The aggregation layer is a thin, stateless composition boundary that enforces the acceptance criterion formula without embedding domain logic. This supports modularity and testability. DP1 and DP4 are overly manual and lack real-time visibility. DP2 couples sensor selection to the metric computation, reducing flexibility. DP3 embeds metering logic into subsystems, creating diffusion and maintenance risk.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source is metered at a single point (or multiple points with known topology). Risk: Distributed or mobile energy sources (e.g., multiple fuel tanks, grid + generator hybrid) may require aggregation logic upstream of the measurement stream.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) is quantifiable at a single or well-defined set of measurement points. Risk: If tonnage is inferred indirectly (e.g., from load cell + distance), measurement error propagates into the ratio; independent validation required.
Assumption: Evaluation period is fixed and known in advance. Risk: If period is dynamic or overlapping, aggregation layer must support windowing and partial-period queries.
Assumption: Acceptance criterion E_target is static. Risk: If E_target varies by worksite, material type, or season, the reporting layer must support parameterized thresholds.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit sensor placement or data logging. Risk: Safety constraints may limit sensor accessibility; mitigation requires early coordination with safety review.

Assumption: Operational policies permit real-time data collection and external reporting. Risk: Data governance or privacy policies may restrict data retention or export; clarify with stakeholders before IM selection.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly define the aggregation layer contract: (1) Energy stream input: cumulative energy (kWh or equivalent) with timestamp; (2) Work stream input: cumulative tonnage with timestamp; (3) Aggregation logic: compute energy_per_ton = ΔEnergy / ΔTonnage over evaluation window; (4) Reporting output: metric value, confidence bounds (if applicable), and pass/fail against E_target. Add a secondary FR to address timestamp synchronization tolerance and sensor validation protocols. This ensures the DP remains solution-neutral while making the composition boundary explicit and testable.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement orchestration and efficiency computation layer that owns both energy consumption and work output measurement streams, their independent sensor/logging pipelines, and the neutral aggregation/reporting logic that computes efficiency ratio.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution exports computed efficiency metrics (energy/work ratio) and disaggregated measurement streams to external telemetry consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives task directives and measurement granularity configuration parameters from tasking authority.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may emit alerts if efficiency metrics fall outside safe operating bounds or measurement streams become inconsistent.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses network link to coordinate distributed sensor data collection (if sensors are remote) and to receive/transmit measurement streams.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is explicitly a measurement and efficiency computation platform. Both energy consumption measurement and work output quantification are core measurement concerns. The neutral aggregation and ratio computation are definitional to NGHE. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and tasking input cross boundaries; sensor/logging pipelines remain internal.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical platform, not a measurement orchestration layer. Energy and work measurement logic would diffuse across vehicle subsystems (powertrain, actuators, sensors). Aggregation and ratio computation belong in a measurement/analytics layer, not vehicle control. High coupling to external reporting systems.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a measurement platform. Energy and work measurement are orthogonal to remote operation concerns. Placing measurement logic here creates tight coupling between operator interface and analytics, violating separation of concerns. Significant diffusion to Vehicle and external systems.

Poor fit. Safety Services owns safety constraints and alerts, not measurement orchestration. While efficiency metrics may inform safety decisions, the measurement streams and ratio computation are not safety-specific concerns. Placing them here conflates measurement with safety policy, creating diffusion to Vehicle sensors and external telemetry consumers.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of DP5 because it is architecturally positioned as the measurement, analytics, and efficiency computation platform. The two independent measurement streams (energy and work) are measurement concerns; the neutral aggregation and ratio computation are analytics concerns. Both are core to NGHE's mission. Sensor/logging pipelines can be encapsulated within NGHE's internal architecture, with external interfaces limited to tasking input (granularity configuration) and telemetry output (computed metrics). This minimizes diffusion and maximizes cohesion.
containment_risks: If energy consumption sensors are tightly coupled to Vehicle powertrain subsystems, NGHE may need to depend on Vehicle-internal sensor APIs, creating hidden coupling. Mitigation: define a sensor abstraction layer within NGHE that decouples from Vehicle implementation details.

If work output quantification requires real-time feedback from Vehicle actuators, latency and synchronization between NGHE and Vehicle could become critical. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry and Network Link interfaces support sufficient bandwidth and low-latency data flow.

If measurement granularity configuration is dynamic and frequent, Tasking interface may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: batch configuration updates or use a dedicated configuration interface derived from Tasking.

If Safety Services requires immediate access to raw measurement streams for safety-critical decisions, NGHE's aggregation layer may introduce unacceptable latency. Mitigation: expose raw streams via SafetyAlert or a dedicated safety interface.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton ≤ E_target` is directly computable from DP5's two independent measurement streams (energy consumption and work output quantification). NGHE Solution's neutral aggregation layer can compute the ratio and log it for evaluation period validation. Measurement granularity configuration via Tasking interface enables traceability. Telemetry export provides audit trail for acceptance verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR requirement maps unambiguously to DP5 (two-stream decomposition + ratio computation) and is owned by NGHE Solution (containment_score 0.92). No competing allocations to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Ownership boundary is architecturally justified: NGHE is positioned as the measurement and analytics platform.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly satisfies FR by decomposing the measurement problem into two independent, composable streams (energy and work) with a neutral aggregation layer. This design avoids tight coupling between energy measurement and work quantification, enabling independent sensor/logging evolution. The ratio computation is explicit and testable. DP5 is sound and complete for the FR.

diffusion_risks: SENSOR ABSTRACTION COUPLING: Energy consumption sensors are likely embedded in Vehicle powertrain subsystems (fuel flow, battery state-of-charge, motor current). NGHE must depend on Vehicle-internal sensor APIs or data buses. If Vehicle sensor interfaces are unstable or proprietary, NGHE's measurement pipeline becomes fragile. Mitigation: define a formal sensor abstraction layer (e.g., standardized energy sensor interface) within NGHE that decouples from Vehicle implementation. Document sensor API contracts explicitly.

WORK OUTPUT LATENCY SYNCHRONIZATION: Work output quantification requires real-time feedback from Vehicle actuators (e.g., load cell, GPS odometry, task completion signals). If NGHE and Vehicle operate on different clock domains or network latencies are high, energy-to-work temporal alignment may degrade. Mitigation: establish time-synchronization protocol between NGHE and Vehicle; define acceptable latency bounds for work output signals; validate synchronization during integration testing.

TASKING CONFIGURATION BOTTLENECK: Measurement granularity configuration (e.g., sampling rate, aggregation window) flows through Tasking interface. If configuration changes are frequent or require low-latency updates, Tasking may become a bottleneck or introduce coupling to tasking authority's operational tempo. Mitigation: batch configuration updates; consider a dedicated configuration interface derived from Tasking; cache configuration locally in NGHE to reduce round-trip latency.

SAFETY-CRITICAL MEASUREMENT LATENCY: Safety Services may require immediate access to raw energy or work measurement streams for safety-critical decisions (e.g., overheat detection, load limit enforcement). NGHE's aggregation layer introduces processing latency. If SafetyAlert interface is the only path to raw streams, safety response time may be unacceptable. Mitigation: expose raw measurement streams via a dedicated low-latency safety interface; document latency SLAs; validate safety-critical paths independently.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score justified). NGHE Solution is the natural architectural home for DP5 because: (1) Both measurement streams (energy, work) are measurement concerns, core to NGHE's mission. (2) Neutral aggregation and ratio computation are analytics concerns, not control or safety logic. (3) Sensor/logging pipelines can be encapsulated within NGHE's internal architecture with well-defined external interfaces (Tasking input, Telemetry output, SafetyAlert output, Network Link for distributed sensors). (4) Minimal diffusion: only configuration and data flow cross boundaries; measurement logic remains cohesive. HOWEVER, the four diffusion risks above must be mitigated through explicit interface contracts and integration testing to prevent hidden coupling to Vehicle, Tasking, and Safety Services.

recommended_revisions: ADD SENSOR ABSTRACTION LAYER: Define a formal sensor interface specification within NGHE (e.g., 'EnergyMeasurementSensor', 'WorkOutputSensor') that abstracts Vehicle-specific sensor implementations. Document sensor API contracts, data types, sampling rates, and error handling. This decouples NGHE from Vehicle subsystem details.

ESTABLISH TIME-SYNCHRONIZATION PROTOCOL: Define a time-sync mechanism between NGHE and Vehicle (e.g., NTP, PTP, or application-level heartbeat) to ensure energy and work measurements are temporally aligned. Document acceptable clock skew and latency bounds. Validate during integration.

FORMALIZE MEASUREMENT GRANULARITY CONFIGURATION: Create a dedicated configuration schema for measurement granularity (sampling rate, aggregation window, reporting interval) that flows through Tasking. Define configuration versioning and caching strategy to minimize latency and coupling.

EXPOSE RAW MEASUREMENT STREAMS FOR SAFETY: Extend SafetyAlert interface to include optional raw energy and work measurement streams (or create a dedicated 'MeasurementStream' interface) for Safety Services to consume without aggregation latency. Document latency SLAs and synchronization guarantees.

DOCUMENT NETWORK LINK REQUIREMENTS: Specify bandwidth, latency, and reliability requirements for Network Link to support distributed sensor data collection and telemetry export. Validate that network infrastructure meets NGHE's measurement timing and accuracy requirements.

ADD MEASUREMENT VALIDATION AND CONSISTENCY CHECKS: Implement internal consistency checks within NGHE (e.g., energy and work streams are temporally aligned, no gaps in measurement windows, sensor health monitoring). Emit alerts via SafetyAlert if consistency is violated. This prevents silent measurement failures.

DEFINE ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA TRACEABILITY: Map acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton ≤ E_target` to specific NGHE outputs (computed ratio, measurement period, sensor configurations used). Document how NGHE logs and exports this data for evaluation period validation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, confirming that FR5 is well-formed and DP5 is sound. However, the DP-IM gate identifies four critical diffusion risks that introduce hidden coupling to Vehicle, Tasking, and Safety Services. Given the HIGH safety criticality of energy measurement (safety-critical latency, sensor abstraction coupling, temporal synchronization), these risks must be mitigated at the current level before proceeding to sub-decomposition. The recommended revisions are architectural in nature and must be reflected in DP5 and IM specifications to prevent downstream integration failures. Specifically: (1) sensor abstraction layer, (2) time-synchronization protocol, (3) measurement granularity configuration formalization, and (4) raw measurement stream exposure for safety are design decisions that belong at the DP5 level, not sub-FR decomposition. Revising DP5 now to incorporate these mitigations will reduce integration risk and clarify ownership boundaries.

revise.why: DP5 must be enriched with explicit architectural patterns to mitigate the four diffusion risks identified in the DP-IM gate. The current DP5 statement ('decompose measurement into two independent streams + neutral aggregation layer') is logically sound but architecturally incomplete. Revisions needed: (1) Add sensor abstraction layer specification to decouple NGHE from Vehicle sensor implementation details. (2) Add time-synchronization protocol requirement to ensure energy and work temporal alignment. (3) Formalize measurement granularity configuration schema and caching strategy to prevent Tasking bottleneck. (4) Add raw measurement stream exposure mechanism for Safety Services to access low-latency data without aggregation latency. These are not sub-requirements; they are design refinements that strengthen DP5's robustness and reduce coupling. Once DP5 is revised to include these patterns, the IM boundary (NGHE Solution) will be more defensible and integration testing will have clear acceptance criteria for interface contracts.

assumptions: Vehicle sensor APIs (fuel flow, battery SOC, motor current) are accessible to NGHE via standardized interfaces or data buses; if proprietary or unstable, sensor abstraction layer becomes critical.
NGHE and Vehicle operate on compatible or synchronizable clock domains; if clock skew exceeds acceptable bounds (TBD), temporal alignment of energy and work streams will degrade.
Tasking interface can support configuration updates with acceptable latency; if Tasking is a bottleneck, dedicated configuration interface may be required.
Safety Services can tolerate aggregation latency for energy/work ratio computation; if safety-critical decisions require raw streams, SafetyAlert interface must expose low-latency measurement data.

Network Link infrastructure supports distributed sensor data collection and telemetry export with sufficient bandwidth and latency characteristics; if network is unreliable or high-latency, measurement accuracy and safety response time may be compromised.
NGHE has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time energy-to-work ratio computation and consistency validation without introducing unacceptable latency.
open_risks: SENSOR ABSTRACTION COUPLING: Vehicle sensor interfaces may be proprietary, unstable, or subject to frequent changes. If NGHE directly depends on Vehicle-internal sensor APIs, measurement pipeline becomes fragile. Mitigation: define formal sensor interface specification (EnergyMeasurementSensor, WorkOutputSensor) within NGHE; document API contracts; validate sensor interface stability during architecture review.

WORK OUTPUT LATENCY SYNCHRONIZATION: Energy and work measurements may be collected on different clock domains or with high network latency. Temporal misalignment could invalidate energy-per-ton ratio. Mitigation: establish time-synchronization protocol (NTP, PTP, or heartbeat); define acceptable clock skew and latency bounds; validate synchronization during integration testing.

TASKING CONFIGURATION BOTTLENECK: Measurement granularity configuration flows through Tasking interface. Frequent configuration changes or low-latency update requirements could create coupling or bottleneck. Mitigation: batch configuration updates; consider dedicated configuration interface; cache configuration locally in NGHE.

SAFETY-CRITICAL MEASUREMENT LATENCY: Safety Services may require immediate access to raw energy/work streams for safety-critical decisions (overheat detection, load limit enforcement). NGHE's aggregation layer introduces processing latency. Mitigation: expose raw measurement streams via dedicated low-latency safety interface; document latency SLAs; validate safety-critical paths independently.

MEASUREMENT CONSISTENCY AND VALIDATION: Silent measurement failures (e.g., sensor dropout, temporal gaps, inconsistent energy-work alignment) could invalidate acceptance criterion verification. Mitigation: implement internal consistency checks within NGHE; emit alerts via SafetyAlert if consistency is violated; document measurement validation logic.

NETWORK LINK RELIABILITY: Distributed sensor data collection and telemetry export depend on Network Link. If network is unreliable, high-latency, or bandwidth-constrained, measurement accuracy and safety response time may be compromised. Mitigation: specify Network Link requirements (bandwidth, latency, reliability); validate infrastructure during integration.
evidence_needed: Formal sensor interface specification (EnergyMeasurementSensor, WorkOutputSensor) with API contracts, data types, sampling rates, error handling, and stability guarantees.
Time-synchronization protocol specification (mechanism, acceptable clock skew, latency bounds, validation approach).
Measurement granularity configuration schema (sampling rate, aggregation window, reporting interval, versioning, caching strategy).
Raw measurement stream interface specification for Safety Services (data types, latency SLAs, synchronization guarantees, safety-critical path validation).
Network Link requirements specification (bandwidth, latency, reliability, jitter) and validation evidence.
Measurement consistency validation logic specification (temporal alignment checks, sensor health monitoring, gap detection, alert criteria).
Acceptance criteria traceability mapping (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target to NGHE outputs, measurement period, sensor configurations, logging/export mechanism).
Integration test plan covering sensor abstraction, time-synchronization, configuration latency, safety-critical measurement latency, and network reliability.
Architecture review evidence confirming sensor interface stability and Vehicle API contracts.
